macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Daf and Review of Previous Discussions on Tumah, Taharah, and Social Stratification

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks and transition to Daf 20, Amud Bet
Overview of the perek's theme: tumah vetaharah and its role in stratifying society
Summary of earlier cases: handwashing for chulin, kavanah at mikvah, and open-ended intent
Post-Churban perspective: shift from chaberim to universal practices like kiddush and brachot

macro_segments.title: The Mishna's Hierarchy of Immersion: Chulin, Maaser, Teruma, and Kodesh

micro_segments.title: Mishna: One who immerses for chulin purposes, what becomes fit for what level
Gemara question: whose position is the Mishna following—the Sages or Rabbi Meir
The seifa (end) of the Mishna regarding clothes of am ha'aretz and their tumah status to different groups
Problem: Maaser is missing from the clothing stratification; Gemara resolves by attributing different parts to different authorities
Rav Acha bar Adda's amendment: inserting a fifth level for those careful with maaser betaharah

macro_segments.title: Chulin Treated with Kodesh-Level Care and Questions of Upward Mobility in Social Hierarchy

micro_segments.title: Rav Mari's statement: if one treats chulin with kodesh-level care, what status does it have
Gemara question: how do we know someone with kodesh-level care belongs to a specific group
Baraita: chulin treated with kodesh care remains as chulin, not elevated to kodesh status
Rabbi Elazar bar Tzadok disputes: such care elevates to teruma level, allowing some upward mobility
Evidence from Yosei ben Yoezer and Yochanan ben Gudgada: their behavior suggests full elevation to kodesh level
Conclusion: the Mishna reflects a position allowing upward mobility through demonstrated care

macro_segments.title: The Role of Conscious Attention and Intent in Maintaining Taharah: Mixed Clothing and Dropped Objects

micro_segments.title: Case of Rabbi Yochanan ben Elazar: a scarf falls and a friend hands it back—becomes tamei
Explanation: someone else's handling means lower care; owner subconsciously relaxes vigilance
Supporting cases: mixed Shabbat/weekday clothes and case from the bathhouse
Gemara's challenge: why does identity or knowledge of the object matter if constant watching is the rule

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Difficulty Reconciling the Consciousness Rule with Cases of Barrels and Provisions

micro_segments.title: Puzzle: basket with figs and shovel—owner only has mind on figs, so shovel becomes tamei
Ravina's explanation: owner protected from av hatumah but not from sheni letumah, allowing selective טומאה
Gemara's תימא: how can one watch things halfway, protecting from some tumot but not others

macro_segments.title: The Extreme Demands of Shemirah: The Case of the Woman Who Wove in Purity But Did Not Guard

micro_segments.title: Woman claims she wove a garment in purity but did not consciously guard it
Rabbi Yishmael's investigation reveals a niddah helped set up the loom frame
Conclusion: active conscious guarding, not retroactive factual assessment, is required for taharah
Rabbi Yishmael's praise for the sages' insight: only belibo lishmor makes it tahor
Second similar case with tablecloth and thread tied with mouth, same principle applies

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Difficulty Reconciling the Consciousness Rule with Cases of Barrels and Provisions (continued)

micro_segments.title: Baraita: barrel of wine watched and assumed to be kept tahor remains tahor even from a distance
Rashi interprets: person watched specifically for things that would make it tamei, not for things that would disqualify

macro_segments.title: The Case of Donkey Drivers and Workers: Exceptional Trust Without Direct Observation

micro_segments.title: Baraita: sealed vessels carried by am ha'aretz workers remain tahor if owner might arrive unexpectedly
Rav Yitzchak Nafcha: they remain tahor because owner made them tahor and maintains threat of inspection
If owner says 'go ahead, I'll catch up,' workers lose vigilance and vessels become tamei
Discussion: seeming contradiction between extreme demands on individual objects and trust in workers
Tosafot's note: takanas hashuk may explain why we allow workers despite normal rules

macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Second Mishna: Stringencies of Kodesh Compared to Teruma

micro_segments.title: Mishna text: multiple stringencies of kodesh beyond those of teruma
Vessels in kodesh must be immersed individually; by teruma multiple vessels can be stacked
By teruma, distinct vessel sections (back, inside, grip) have separate tumah status; not so for kodesh
Carrying av hatumah on shoulder while carrying teruma: permitted with pottery barrel; not for kodesh
Clothes of teruma-eaters are midras for kodesh-eaters; different status from teruma
Garment preparation for kodesh: must untie, dry, immerse, then retie; simpler process for teruma
New vessels made in purity still require immersion before first use for kodesh; not for teruma
Vessel treats its contents as one unit for kodesh contamination; separate items for teruma
Fourth-level derivation of tumah disqualifies for kodesh; only third level for teruma
One hand becoming tamei: other hand stays tahor for teruma if watched; both hands wash for kodesh
Dry food eaten with tamei hands: permitted for teruma; prohibited for kodesh
Onein and mechusar kipurim require immersion before kodesh; difference from teruma

topical_tags.term: Chulin vs Maaser vs Teruma vs Kodesh hierarchy
Midras (seat of zav/zavah as av hatumah)
Rabbi Elazar bar Tzadok upward mobility view
Yochanan ben Gudgada—al taharas hakodesh example
Rabbi Yochanan ben Elazar—fallen scarf case
Deriving tumah levels (rishon, sheni, shlishi, revii)
Vessel as av hatumah vs distinct sections
